epilogue.definition: A short section at the end of a book that adds final thoughts.
epilogue.example: The epilogue wrapped up the story beautifully.

comedian.definition: A person whose job is to make people laugh.
comedian.example: The comedian told a funny joke about parenting.

envisaged.definition: Imagined or expected something to be a certain way.
envisaged.example: The trip was not what I had envisaged.

picnic.definition: A meal eaten outside, usually on a blanket.
picnic.example: We had a lovely picnic by the lake.

aside.definition: To one side; away from you.
aside.example: Put aside your worries and relax.

blocks.definition: Things that prevent you from doing or feeling something.
blocks.example: Emotional blocks can stop you from connecting with others.

inhibit.definition: To stop or slow down something from happening.
inhibit.example: Fear can inhibit your ability to try new things.

warmth.definition: A friendly and caring feeling toward someone.
warmth.example: Her warmth made everyone feel welcome.

acceptance.definition: Being willing to receive someone or something without trying to change them.
acceptance.example: Children need acceptance from their parents.

physical.definition: Related to the body rather than the mind.
physical.example: Physical touch is important for babies.

presence.definition: Being there with someone, giving them your attention.
presence.example: Your presence means more than any gift.

harmonious.definition: Peaceful; with everyone getting along well.
harmonious.example: They created a harmonious home for their children.

environment.definition: The conditions and surroundings where someone lives.
environment.example: A safe environment helps children learn.

differences.definition: Ways in which people or things are not the same.
differences.example: They learned to respect their differences.

soothing.definition: Making someone feel calm and less worried or upset.
soothing.example: The mother's soothing voice calmed the baby.

attention.definition: The act of watching, listening, or thinking about something carefully.
attention.example: Children need a lot of attention from their parents.

situations.definition: Particular sets of conditions or circumstances at a certain time.

express.definition: To show or tell others what you think or feel.
express.example: It is important to express your feelings.

validate.definition: To show that someone's feelings or opinions are understood and accepted.
validate.example: A good listener will validate your feelings.

attempt.example: She made an attempt to learn the piano.

rescue.definition: To save someone from a dangerous or difficult situation.
rescue.example: Parents should not always rescue their children from problems.

brainstorm.definition: To think of many different ideas or solutions quickly.
brainstorm.example: Let's brainstorm ways to solve this problem.

boundaries.definition: Rules or limits that show what is acceptable behavior.
boundaries.example: Setting boundaries helps children feel safe.

defining.definition: Explaining clearly what something means or what you stand for.
defining.example: She is defining her own values as a parent.

non-defensive.definition: Not trying to protect yourself from blame; open to hearing criticism.
non-defensive.example: Being non-defensive helps you learn from mistakes.

repair.definition: To fix something that was broken or damaged.
repair.example: It takes effort to repair a broken relationship.

owning.definition: Accepting that something is your fault or responsibility.
owning.example: Owning your mistakes makes you a stronger person.

dynamics.definition: The way people interact and the forces that drive their behavior together.
dynamics.example: Family dynamics change when a new baby arrives.

cooperation.definition: Working together with others to achieve something.
cooperation.example: The project was a success thanks to cooperation.

collaboration.definition: Working with someone else to create or produce something together.
collaboration.example: Their collaboration resulted in a wonderful book.

treasure.definition: To value something very highly; to care for it deeply.
treasure.example: Treasure the time you spend with your family.

authentic.definition: Real and genuine; true to who you really are.
authentic.example: Children respond well to authentic emotions.

concentrate.definition: To focus all your attention on one thing.
concentrate.example: Concentrate on the problem, not just the child.

general.definition: Applying to all or most cases, not specific.
general.example: These are general rules for good parenting.

apply.definition: To be relevant or true in a situation.
apply.example: These rules apply to children of all ages.

whatever.definition: No matter what; regardless of what.
whatever.example: I will support you whatever happens.

amazing.example: It is amazing how fast children grow.

despite.definition: Even though something else is happening; regardless of.
despite.example: Despite the rain, we had a good time.

dump.definition: To put or throw something onto someone carelessly and unfairly.
dump.example: Don't dump your anger on your children.

rush.definition: To do something too quickly without enough care.
rush.example: Don't rush the children; let them take their time.

unavailable.definition: Not able to be reached or not present for someone who needs you.
unavailable.example: The parent was emotionally unavailable to the child.

standpoint.definition: A particular way of seeing or thinking about something; a point of view.
standpoint.example: Try to see things from your child's standpoint.

overidentify.definition: To connect too strongly with someone else's feelings, losing your own separate view.
overidentify.example: Parents can overidentify with their child and lose perspective.

merge.definition: To blend together and lose the difference between two things.
merge.example: Try not to merge your identity with your child's.

separate.definition: To become independent; to exist apart from someone else.
separate.example: Children need to learn to separate from their parents.

bonded.definition: Joined together by a strong emotional connection.
bonded.example: The mother and baby bonded quickly after birth.

reassuring.definition: Making you feel less worried; comforting.
reassuring.example: It is reassuring to know you can fix mistakes.

ruptures.definition: Breaks or tears in a relationship caused by hurt or misunderstanding.
ruptures.example: Ruptures in relationships can be repaired with honesty.

forgiving.definition: Willing to stop being angry at someone who did wrong.
forgiving.example: Being forgiving of yourself is important as a parent.

brave.definition: Having the courage to face something difficult or scary.
brave.example: It takes being brave to admit your mistakes.

honest.definition: Telling the truth; not lying or hiding things.
honest.example: Be honest with your children about your feelings.
